2.1.title: Three participles framing one verb उवाच
2.1.body: Sañjaya stacks three present/past participles agreeing with तम् (Arjuna, द्वितीया एकवचन): कृपयाविष्टम् (instrumental कृपया + past participle of आ-विश्), अश्रुपूर्णाकुलेक्षणम् (बहुव्रीहि compound — 'whose eyes are filled with tears and agitated'), and विषीदन्तम् (शतृ-कृदन्त, present active participle of वि-सद्). Only उवाच (√वच्, परोक्ष-भूत/लिट्, 3sg) is finite — everything else is adjectival pile-up around the object.

2.1.title: मधुसूदनः — epithet doing structural work
2.1.body: Naming Kṛṣṇa as मधुसूदनः ('slayer of Madhu') as the nominative subject of उवाच is not decoration. Sañjaya signals that the same one who once destroyed an असुर is now speaking — the speaker has authority over despair. The compound is an उपपद-तत्पुरुष (मधुं सूदयतीति), where the agent-suffix ल्यु (= -अन) attaches to the verbal stem सूदय् via the नन्द्यादि class (Pāṇini 3.1.134).

2.2.title: कुतः opens an interrogative cascade
2.2.body: Kṛṣṇa's first word is the interrogative adverb कुतः ('whence?'), governing समुपस्थितम् (past passive participle of सम्-उप-स्था, agreeing with कश्मलम् — neuter प्रथमा एकवचन). त्वा is the enclitic accusative of युष्मद् ('to you'). Three adjectives — अनार्यजुष्टम् (तत्पुरुष: 'frequented by non-Āryas'), अस्वर्ग्यम् (नञ्-तत्पुरुष), अकीर्तिकरम् (उपपद-तत्पुरुष: 'fame-destroying') — all agree with कश्मलम्.

2.2.title: विषमे — locative of crisis
2.2.body: विषमे is सप्तमी एकवचन of विषम ('uneven, crisis-moment'), locating *when* this faintness arose — at the worst possible moment. The vocative अर्जुन closes the verse, mirroring the opening कुतः: Kṛṣṇa frames the question and names the addressee, refusing to let Arjuna hide behind philosophical posture.

2.3.title: Two imperatives + one prohibitive aorist + one absolutive
2.3.body: The verse stacks three commands of differing morphology. गमः (√गम्, 2sg) is *not* लोट् but the augmentless aorist (लुङ् without अ-) used in the मा स्म prohibitive — 'do not yield'. उत्तिष्ठ (उद्-√स्था, 2sg) is the genuine लोट् imperative — 'rise!'. त्यक्त्वा is the absolutive (क्त्वान्त) of √त्यज् — 'having abandoned'. The absolutive subordinates: abandon weakness, *then* rise.

2.3.title: क्लैब्यम् — the abstract noun as moral diagnosis
2.3.body: क्लैब्यम् is a भाव-वाचक abstract noun derived from क्लीब (eunuch) via -य suffix — not literal but psychological: 'eunuch-ness', impotence of will. Kṛṣṇa pairs it with हृदयदौर्बल्यम् (कर्मधारय: 'weakness of heart'), the कारण behind the symptom. नैतत्त्वय्युपपद्यते — न + एतत् + त्वयि + उपपद्यते — 'this does not befit you' (लट्, आत्मनेपद).

2.4.title: Two vocatives, not two objects
2.4.body: मधुसूदन and अरिसूदन (both सम्बोधन एकवचन of तत्पुरुष agent compounds) look like accusatives but are vocatives — Kṛṣṇa addressed twice in one verse, framing the question. The real द्वितीया objects are भीष्मम् and द्रोणम्. प्रतियोत्स्यामि (प्रति-√युध्, लृट्, उत्तम-पुरुष एकवचन) — the -ष्य- infix and the prefix lock in 'I shall fight against'.

2.4.title: पूजार्हौ — dual adjective for two elders
2.4.body: पूजार्हौ is द्विवचन (dual) of पूजार्ह ('worthy of worship', उपपद-तत्पुरुष), agreeing with the *combined* pair भीष्मम् and द्रोणम् — Sanskrit's dual handles 'these two venerable men' as one grammatical unit. इषुभिः (तृतीया बहुवचन of इषु, 'arrow') is the karaṇa — the instrument of an act Arjuna cannot stomach performing on the worship-worthy.

2.5.title: Three verb-shapes, one finite भुञ्जीय
2.5.body: The verse's grammatical anchor is भुञ्जीय (√भुज्, विधिलिङ्, 1sg आत्मनेपद): 'should I enjoy?' The -यात्/-ीय signature marks the mood. भोक्तुम् is तुमुन्-infinitive ('to eat'); हत्वा and अहत्वा are absolutives (with and without negation). Only the optative is finite — Arjuna's rhetorical horror is *grammatically* baked into विधिलिङ्'s deliberative tone.

2.5.title: महानुभावान् गुरून् — सामानाधिकरण्य in द्वितीया
2.5.body: Adjective महानुभावान् ('great-souled', बहुव्रीहि) and noun गुरून् agree in द्वितीया बहुवचन पुल्लिङ्ग — सामानाधिकरण्य ('co-referentiality'). रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् ('blood-smeared', past participle of प्र-√दिह्) similarly agrees with भोगान्. Each adjective's case ending is the receipt that says: I belong to *that* noun.

2.6.title: Twin optatives in one sentence
2.6.body: जयेम (√जि, विधिलिङ्, 1pl) and जयेयुः (3pl) frame the alternatives — 'whether we should conquer them or they us'. कतरत् ('which of two') is the interrogative pronoun in नपुंसक प्रथमा एकवचन agreeing with the implied better-option (गरीयः). विद्मः (√विद्, लट्, 1pl) is the matrix verb — 'we do not know'.

2.6.title: जिजीविषामः — desiderative morphology
2.6.body: जिजीविषामः is the सन्नन्त (desiderative) of √जीव् + लट् 1pl: 'we do not wish to live'. The redoubling जि- and the -इष- suffix are the desiderative signature. यान् हत्वा — relative pronoun in द्वितीया बहुवचन + absolutive — 'having killed whom (we would not wish to live)'. The grammar inverts: the relative clause comes first, the antecedent ते is the predicate.

2.7.title: Three self-diagnoses as compounds
2.7.body: Arjuna labels himself with three long bahuvrīhis: कार्पण्यदोषोपहतस्वभावः ('whose nature is struck down by the defect of pity'), धर्मसम्मूढचेताः ('whose mind is bewildered about dharma'), and शिष्यस्ते (शिष्यः + ते — 'your disciple'). Each compound is a confessional, the morphology itself is the surrender.

2.7.title: शाधि — the rare 2sg imperative of √शास्
2.7.body: शाधि is the लोट् 2sg of √शास् ('teach, instruct') — the irregular root drops to शा- before the imperative ending -धि. प्रपन्नम् is the past passive participle of प्र-√पद् agreeing with माम् — 'me, who am surrendered to you'. The single verse contains the शिक्षा-शरणागति formula: I am your disciple, instruct me, I have come for refuge.

2.8.title: Optative अपनुद्यात् inside the relative
2.8.body: The matrix verb is प्रपश्यामि (प्र-√दृश्, लट् 1sg, negated by न हि) — 'I do not foresee'. Inside the relative clause, अपनुद्यात् (अप-√नुद्, विधिलिङ् 3sg) is the optative — 'that which would dispel'. यत्...तत् correlative is implicit; ममापनुद्यात् = मम (षष्ठी) + अप-नुद्यात् ('would dispel mine').

2.8.title: Two-step concessive with अवाप्य
2.8.body: अवाप्य is the ल्यप्-absolutive of अव-√आप् — 'even having obtained'. The objects it governs are massive: राज्यम् qualified by असपत्नम् ('without rival', बहुव्रीहि), ऋद्धम् ('prosperous'), भूमौ (locative), and the upper bound सुराणाम् आधिपत्यम् ('sovereignty even over gods', षष्ठी + abstract noun). Even *this* would not dry up his grief (उच्छोषणम् इन्द्रियाणाम्).

2.9.title: Three -केश epithets locked in one śloka
2.9.body: Sañjaya names every speaker by epithet: हृषीकेशम् (Kṛṣṇa, द्वितीया), गुडाकेशः (Arjuna, प्रथमा — 'lord of sleep' / 'thick-haired'), and गोविन्दम् (Kṛṣṇa again, द्वितीया). The threefold renaming sets up the inversion: the lord of senses is addressed by one who has conquered sleep, declaring he will *not* act.

2.9.title: बभूव ह — perfect with the particle of finality
2.9.body: बभूव is लिट् (perfect) of √भू, 3sg; the particle ह marks completed, witnessed past — 'he became silent, indeed'. योत्स्ये is लृट् आत्मनेपद 1sg of √युध् ('I shall not fight'). उक्त्वा is absolutive — Sañjaya sequences two speech-acts (एवम् उक्त्वा...उक्त्वा) before the silence lands. Three verbal forms; the perfect is the seal.

2.10.title: प्रहसन् इव — laughing, as it were
2.10.body: प्रहसन् is शतृ-कृदन्त (present active participle, प्रथमा एकवचन of प्र-√हस्), agreeing with हृषीकेशः. The particle इव softens it — 'as if smiling'. The smile is the pedagogical pivot: Kṛṣṇa does not console, he gently mocks. विषीदन्तम् (शतृ of वि-√सद्) agrees with तम्, mirroring 2.1's structure — the despondent one is again the object of the speaking.

2.10.title: Locative compound सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये
2.10.body: सेनयोः is षष्ठी द्विवचन ('of the two armies'), उभयोः agrees with it (also षष्ठी द्विवचन of उभय), and मध्ये is सप्तमी एकवचन — 'in the middle of the two armies'. The spatial geometry stays exact: the teaching happens *between* the lines, in no-man's land. वचः (नपुंसक प्रथमा) is the object of उवाच, qualified by इदम्.

2.11.title: Past tense अन्वशोचः with object
2.11.body: अन्वशोचः is the लङ् 2sg of अनु-√शुच् ('you have grieved over'), with augment अ- and ending -ः. The object is अशोच्यान् — verbal adjective from शुच् with कृत्य-suffix -य ('not to be grieved over'), द्वितीया बहुवचन पुल्लिङ्ग. The same root anchors the negation in pāda 2: नानुशोचन्ति पण्डिताः (लट् 3pl) — 'the wise do not grieve'.

2.11.title: Two participles agreeing in द्वितीया बहुवचन
2.11.body: गतासून् ('whose breath has gone', बहुव्रीहि — गताः असवः येषाम्) and अगतासून् ('whose breath has not gone') both stand in द्वितीया बहुवचन, the objects of अनुशोचन्ति. प्रज्ञावादान् is similarly accusative plural ('words of wisdom') — Arjuna grieves and speechifies; the wise do neither.

2.12.title: Past, future, and the unbroken self
2.12.body: One past, one future, both negated: न जातु न आसम् (√अस्, लङ् 1sg — 'never was I not') anchors the past; न च एव न भविष्यामः (√भू, लृट् 1pl — 'nor shall we not be') anchors the future. The particles त्वेव and च एव lock emphasis. The grammar of doubled negation — 'not non-existent past' + 'not non-existent future' — yields unbroken being across time.

2.12.title: अतः परम् — the temporal adverbial
2.12.body: अतः परम् ('hereafter, beyond this') is ablative + adverbial accusative, locating future continuity. जनाधिपाः (कर्मधारय? — actually तत्पुरुष: जनानाम् अधिपाः 'lords of people') in प्रथमा बहुवचन makes the plural concrete — the kings before us are part of the same eternal set, not abstractions.

2.13.title: देहिनः — the embodied one as षष्ठी subject
2.13.body: देहिनः is षष्ठी एकवचन of देहिन् ('possessor of body'), the technical term Kṛṣṇa repeats throughout the chapter for the आत्मन्. यथा...तथा is the standard correlative; the यथा-clause names three states (कौमारं, यौवनं, जरा — all प्रथमा एकवचन abstract nouns) and the तथा-clause names the fourth: देहान्तरप्राप्तिः ('the obtaining of another body', तत्पुरुष षष्ठी).

2.13.title: मुह्यति — the verbal litmus test
2.13.body: धीरः ('the steady one', प्रथमा एकवचन) is the subject; तत्र is locative-adverb ('therein'); न मुह्यति is लट् 3sg of √मुह् ('he is not deluded'). Sanskrit drops the explicit copula and lets the finite verb sit at the end — the अन्वय restores: धीरः तत्र न मुह्यति. The diagnostic is non-delusion in the face of transition.

2.14.title: मात्रास्पर्शाः — the technical compound
2.14.body: मात्रास्पर्शाः is तत्पुरुष ('contacts of the material elements'), प्रथमा बहुवचन subject. The predicate शीतोष्णसुखदुःखदाः is an उपपद-तत्पुरुष with a द्वन्द्व inside — शीत-उष्ण-सुख-दुःख (the dvandva) + -द (agent suffix from √दा, 'giver of...'). The pile of dualities is folded into one compound — the grammar mimics the message: these are all one phenomenon.

2.14.title: तितिक्षस्व — imperative of endurance
2.14.body: तितिक्षस्व is the सन्नन्त (desiderative) base of √तिज् in लोट् आत्मनेपद 2sg — 'endure, bear them'. The desiderative semantics here is lexicalized ('to wish-to-cut' → 'to bear'). The object तान् refers back to मात्रास्पर्शाः; आगमापायिनः ('coming-and-going', उपपद-तत्पुरुष + -इन्) and अनित्याः are predicate adjectives.

2.15.title: Triple-accusative construction with व्यथयन्ति
2.15.body: एते (प्रथमा बहुवचन subject — these contacts) व्यथयन्ति (causative of √व्यथ्, लट् 3pl) पुरुषम् (द्वितीया एकवचन direct object). The relative pronoun यम् is the द्वितीया एकवचन head, picked up by सः in pāda 4. समदुःखसुखम् is बहुव्रीहि ('to whom pleasure and pain are equal') agreeing with यम् पुरुषम्.

2.15.title: Dative of purpose अमृतत्वाय
2.15.body: अमृतत्वाय is चतुर्थी एकवचन of the abstract noun अमृतत्व ('immortality') — the dative of purpose ('for/towards immortality'). कल्पते (√कॢप्, लट् आत्मनेपद 3sg) governs the dative: 'he is fit for'. The verse's grammar gives the formula: equanimity (बहुव्रीहि) → fitness (कल्पते) → immortality (चतुर्थी).

2.16.title: Two genitives of opposing terms
2.16.body: असतः and सतः are both षष्ठी एकवचन (of the non-existent / of the existent), with the matrix verb विद्यते (√विद् दिवादि, आत्मनेपद लट् 3sg — 'is found, exists'). Pāda 1: न असतः भावः विद्यते. Pāda 2 inverts: न अभावः सतः विद्यते. The neat chiastic reflection in Sanskrit case morphology is the philosophical claim.

2.16.title: तत्त्वदर्शिभिः — the karaṇa-tṛtīyā of the seers
2.16.body: तत्त्वदर्शिभिः is तृतीया बहुवचन of तत्त्वदर्शिन् ('seer of the real', उपपद + -इन्), the agent of the passive दृष्टः (past passive participle). अनयोः उभयोः ('of these two', षष्ठी द्विवचन) — refers to असत् and सत्. अन्तः ('the limit, conclusion') is the प्रथमा subject. The grammar: seers have seen the limit-distinction.

2.17.title: अविनाशि as नपुंसक adjective of तत्
2.17.body: अविनाशि is नपुंसकलिङ्ग प्रथमा एकवचन (in -इन् stem, neuter), agreeing with तत् ('that'). विद्धि is the लोट् 2sg of √विद् ('know!'). The relative clause येन सर्वम् इदम् ततम् uses तृतीया एकवचन of यद् + the past passive participle ततम् of √तन् ('stretched, pervaded'). The whole-cosmos-is-pervaded statement is grammatically a relative clause modifying तत्.

2.17.title: Genitive अव्ययस्य with infinitive कर्तुम्
2.17.body: विनाशम् कर्तुम् ('to do the destruction' — द्वितीया + तुमुन्) is the infinitive complement of अर्हति (√अर्ह्, लट् 3sg). अव्ययस्य अस्य is षष्ठी एकवचन ('of this imperishable'). न कश्चित् (प्रथमा 'no one') is the subject. The classical 'no-one is-able to-do destruction of-the-imperishable' construction — Sanskrit slots Genitive→Infinitive→Verb.

2.18.title: Three contrasting षष्ठी एकवचन of the self
2.18.body: नित्यस्य, अनाशिनः, अप्रमेयस्य — three genitives in series, all qualifying शरीरिणः ('the embodied one', षष्ठी एकवचन of शरीरिन्). The देहाः (प्रथमा बहुवचन subject) are predicated as अन्तवन्तः ('having an end', -वत् possessive suffix). The grammar polarizes: bodies have end; their owner does not.

2.18.title: Imperative युध्यस्व closing the kṣatriya argument
2.18.body: तस्मात् ('therefore', ablative-adverb) connects metaphysics to action. युध्यस्व is लोट् आत्मनेपद 2sg of √युध् — the second-person command sealed by the vocative भारत. This is the chapter's structural pivot: the body/self distinction is not for contemplation but for combat.

2.19.title: Two relative pronouns, one verdict
2.19.body: यः...यश्च — both प्रथमा एकवचन of यद्, picking up the two errant views. Their finite verbs: वेत्ति (√विद्, लट् 3sg) and मन्यते (√मन्, लट् आत्मनेपद 3sg). The objects are participial: हन्तारम् (तृच्-कृदन्त agent noun in द्वितीया एकवचन of √हन्, 'slayer') and हतम् (past passive participle in द्वितीया एकवचन, 'slain').

2.19.title: Dual verb विजानीतः for the two errant ones
2.19.body: उभौ तौ ('those two', द्विवचन प्रथमा) governs विजानीतः (वि-√ज्ञा, लट् 3rd-dual परस्मैपद, athematic) — Sanskrit's dual is alive and well. The closing न हन्ति न हन्यते pairs active and passive of √हन् in लट् 3sg — neither slayer nor slain. The grammar refuses both grammatical voices for the आत्मन्.

2.20.title: Six negated verbs and one bahuvrīhi pile-up
2.20.body: जायते, म्रियते, भूत्वा भविता, हन्यते, हन्यमाने — the verse is a verb-cluster. भविता (√भू, लुट् periphrastic future 3sg — rare!), हन्यते is the karmaṇi लट् 3sg of √हन्, हन्यमाने is the present passive participle in सप्तमी एकवचन (locative absolute: 'when the body is being killed').

2.20.title: Four predicate adjectives in प्रथमा एकवचन
2.20.body: अजः, नित्यः, शाश्वतः, पुराणः — four adjectives stack as predicates of अयम् (the self). अजः is नञ्-तत्पुरुष ('unborn'); पुराणः derives from पुरा + -त्न/-तन (the suffix पुराण per Pāṇini 5.3.22 vārttika, 'of ancient time'). The four are deliberately ordered: not-born, eternal, perpetual, ancient — past-and-future stretched into negations of birth.

2.21.title: वेद with द्वितीया triple-accusative
2.21.body: वेद is लिट् (perfect with present meaning) 3sg of √विद् ('he knows'). Three द्वितीया एकवचन objects: अविनाशिनम्, नित्यम्, अजम् अव्ययम् — agreeing with एनम् ('this [self]'). The यः...सः structure makes it a relative-correlative: 'he who knows...how does that man...'.

2.21.title: घातयति vs. हन्ति — causative and simple
2.21.body: Two finite verbs in the question: घातयति (णिजन्त-causative of √हन्, 'he causes to be slain', लट् 3sg) and हन्ति (simple लट् 3sg, 'he slays'). The interrogatives कथम् ('how') and कम् ('whom', द्वितीया एकवचन of किम्) lock both verbs to impossibility. Sanskrit grammar of causation itself is the philosophical knife.

2.22.title: One absolutive + one finite verb mirrored in each half
2.22.body: Each half has one absolutive and one finite verb. यथा-half: विहाय (absolutive of वि-√हा, 'having abandoned') + गृह्णाति (√ग्रह्, लट् 3sg, 'takes'), with नरः (प्रथमा एकवचन) as subject. तथा-half: same absolutive विहाय + संयाति (सम्-√या, लट् 3sg, 'goes to'), with देही (देहिन्, प्रथमा एकवचन) as subject. The doubled absolutive is the metaphor's hinge.

2.22.title: जीर्णानि / नवानि — adjectives in नपुंसक बहुवचन
2.22.body: वासांसि is नपुंसक प्रथमा बहुवचन of वासस् ('garment'); जीर्णानि (worn out) and नवानि (new) agree. The mirror in pāda 2: शरीराणि...जीर्णानि...अन्यानि नवानि — same morphology, different lexeme. The grammar makes the metaphor airtight: clothes and bodies share gender and number, share the verb-pair विहाय/गृह्णाति.

2.23.title: Four elements as four agents of failed action
2.23.body: Four प्रथमा एकवचन subjects: शस्त्राणि (नपुंसक बहुवचन — collectively treated), पावकः (fire), आपः (waters — only-plural noun), मारुतः (wind). Four lat verbs: छिन्दन्ति (√छिद् 3pl), दहति (√दह् 3sg), क्लेदयन्ति (causative of √क्लिद्, 3pl 'wet'), शोषयति (causative of √शुष्, 3sg 'dries'). Each negated by न (with च for joins).

2.23.title: एनम् as the recurring द्वितीया एकवचन target
2.23.body: एनम् ('him/this') is repeated thrice in this single verse, all द्वितीया एकवचन referring to आत्मन्. The pronoun एतद् in its enclitic accusative form एनम् is grammatically restricted to anaphora — it cannot start a clause. The verse's anaphoric weight: four elements, one undamaged referent, repeated four times.

2.24.title: Four कृत्य-pratyaya adjectives in series
2.24.body: अच्छेद्यः (न + छिद् + -य 'not to be cut'), अदाह्यः (-य of √दह्), अक्लेद्यः (-य of √क्लिद्), अशोष्यः — all four are कृत्य-कृदन्त (gerundive/passive potential), प्रथमा एकवचन, agreeing with अयम् (the self). The grammar inverts 2.23: there the elements *do not* act; here the self *cannot be acted upon*. Voice mirrors substance.

2.24.title: Three more adjectives wrapped around स्थाणुः
2.24.body: नित्यः, सर्वगतः, स्थाणुः, अचलः, सनातनः — five more प्रथमा एकवचन adjectives. स्थाणुः ('fixed, like a post') is the concrete metaphor in the middle; सनातनः ('primordial', -तन suffix) closes the verse. The pile of synonyms is the point — Sanskrit prefers explicit redundancy of adjectives in चतुष्पदा construction.

2.25.title: Three more adjectives ending in -य कृत्य
2.25.body: अव्यक्तः ('un-manifest', past passive participle of व्यञ्ज् with नञ्), अचिन्त्यः (-य कृत्य 'unthinkable'), अविकार्यः (वि-√कृ + -य 'not modifiable'). The उच्यते (passive लट् 3sg of √वच्) at the end of pāda 2 acknowledges the limit: 'is *said* to be' — the predicate is verbal-report, not direct grasp.

2.25.title: विदित्वा — absolutive bridging to imperative
2.25.body: विदित्वा (absolutive of √विद् 'having known') governs एनम् (द्वितीया). एवम् ('thus') refers back to the three adjectives. नानुशोचितुम् अर्हसि — the तुमुन्-infinitive (अनु-√शुच् + तुम्) complementing अर्हसि (√अर्ह्, लट् 2sg) — 'you are not entitled to grieve'. The तस्मात् sequence: thus-knowing → not-fitting to grieve.

2.26.title: Conditional अथ चेत्...तथापि construction
2.26.body: अथ च एनम् ('and now this one' — द्वितीया) is the protasis-subject; मन्यसे (√मन्, लट् आत्मनेपद 2sg) is the verb of opining. नित्यजातम् and नित्यम् वा मृतम् are द्वितीया एकवचन predicates (तत्पुरुष + past passive participle). तथापि ('even then') begins the apodosis.

2.26.title: नैवं शोचितुम् अर्हसि — second occurrence
2.26.body: The verse repeats the अर्हसि-construction from 2.25 verbatim — Kṛṣṇa anticipates: even granting your premise (eternal birth-and-death), the conclusion (don't grieve) follows. The vocative महाबाहो ('great-armed one', सम्बोधन of बहुव्रीहि) is the rhetorical jab — addressing him by his warrior epithet while reasoning him out of paralysis.

2.27.title: ध्रुवः as predicate adjective
2.27.body: Two parallel ध्रुवः-statements: ध्रुवः मृत्युः जातस्य (षष्ठी एकवचन — 'certain is death of the born') and ध्रुवम् जन्म मृतस्य ('certain is birth of the dead'). The chiasmus is perfect: जात/मृत/जन्म/मृत. The genitives establish ownership of inevitabilities. हि ('for, indeed') marks the reasoning.

2.27.title: अपरिहार्ये अर्थे — locative absolute of finality
2.27.body: अपरिहार्ये is सप्तमी एकवचन of the कृत्य-adjective ('unavoidable'), agreeing with अर्थे ('matter, situation'). The locative names the domain in which grief is illegitimate. The तस्मात्...न शोचितुम् अर्हसि formula repeats — the third time in three verses. Repetition is the chapter's structural device.

2.28.title: Three bahuvrīhi compounds for all beings
2.28.body: अव्यक्तादीनि ('whose beginning is unmanifest'), व्यक्तमध्यानि ('whose middle is manifest'), अव्यक्तनिधनानि ('whose end is unmanifest') — three बहुव्रीहि agreeing with भूतानि (नपुंसक प्रथमा बहुवचन). The triple compound encloses the timeline of all existence in three case-bound predicate phrases.

2.28.title: का परिदेवना — interrogative + abstract noun
2.28.body: का is स्त्रीलिंग प्रथमा एकवचन of किम्, agreeing with परिदेवना ('lamentation', स्त्रीलिंग — feminine -आ stem). तत्र ('there') refers to the metaphysical situation. The rhetorical question — 'what lamentation?' — is grammatically a copular sentence with implicit अस्ति, the interrogative pronoun making the assertion.

2.29.title: आश्चर्यवत् adverb stacked three times
2.29.body: आश्चर्यवत् is an indeclinable -वत् adverbial form ('as a wonder'), used thrice as manner-adverb modifying three verbs: पश्यति, वदति, शृणोति (all √दृश्/वच्/श्रु, लट् 3sg). कश्चित् ('some-one') and अन्यः ('another') alternate as subjects — Sanskrit's way of saying 'one...another...yet another'.

2.29.title: श्रुत्वा + वेद न — the closing failure
2.29.body: श्रुत्वा is absolutive of √श्रु ('having heard'). अपि ('even') concessive. वेद is लिट् 3sg of √विद् with present meaning ('knows'); न च एव कश्चित् — 'and indeed no one [knows]'. The four verbs of cognition (see, speak, hear, know) climax in negation: even hearing about the आत्मन्, nobody truly knows it.

2.30.title: देही अवध्यः — bahuvrīhi-like double prafia
2.30.body: देही (प्रथमा एकवचन of देहिन् — the embodied one) is the subject; अवध्यः ('not-to-be-killed', कृत्य of √वध् with नञ्) is the predicate adjective. नित्यम् is the adverbial accusative ('always'). देहे (सप्तमी एकवचन) and सर्वस्य (षष्ठी एकवचन of सर्व) together: 'in the body of all'.

2.30.title: तस्मात्...न शोचितुम् अर्हसि — closing the Sāṅkhya section
2.30.body: The तस्मात् + न शोचितुम् अर्हसि formula appears for the fourth time (2.25, 2.26, 2.27, 2.30), structurally closing Kṛṣṇa's eternal-self argument. सर्वाणि भूतानि is द्वितीया बहुवचन ('regarding all beings') — adverbial accusative of relation. The verse seals one block before pivoting to the kṣatriya-duty section in 2.31.

2.31.title: स्वधर्मम् अवेक्ष्य — absolutive pivot to dharma argument
2.31.body: अवेक्ष्य is the ल्यप्-absolutive of अव-√ईक्ष् ('having considered'); its object स्वधर्मम् (द्वितीया एकवचन of तत्पुरुष 'own-duty'). अपि here is concessive ('also/even'). The matrix verb is अर्हसि (लट् 2sg) with infinitive विकम्पितुम् (तुमुन् of वि-√कम्प् 'to waver') — 'you ought not waver'.

2.31.title: धर्म्यात् युद्धात् — ablative of comparison
2.31.body: धर्म्यात् युद्धात् (पञ्चमी एकवचन — 'than a righteous battle') is the ablative of comparison governed by श्रेयः ('better', comparative -ईयस् of प्रशस्य). क्षत्रियस्य (षष्ठी एकवचन — 'for a kṣatriya') is the genitive of beneficiary. The full अन्वय: 'than a righteous battle, nothing better exists for a kṣatriya'.

2.32.title: Two past participles agreeing with युद्धम्
2.32.body: उपपन्नम् (past passive participle of उप-√पद् 'arrived, presented') and अपावृतम् (past passive participle of अप-आ-√वृ 'opened wide') — both नपुंसक एकवचन, agreeing with स्वर्गद्वारम् (तत्पुरुष: 'gate of heaven'). यदृच्छया is तृतीया एकवचन ('by chance, unbidden') — adverbial instrumental.

2.32.title: ईदृशम् युद्धम् — the demonstrative-adjective tag
2.32.body: लभन्ते (√लभ्, लट् आत्मनेपद 3pl) is the matrix verb; क्षत्रियाः (प्रथमा बहुवचन) the subject. ईदृशम् ('such-like', a त्यदादि pronoun-adjective in द्वितीया एकवचन) modifies युद्धम् — 'such a war'. सुखिनः ('happy ones', तत्पुरुष/मतुप्) is a predicate adjective: 'fortunate are the kṣatriyas who obtain such a war'.

2.33.title: Future conditional with चेत् + करिष्यसि
2.33.body: अथ चेत् introduces the conditional. करिष्यसि is लृट् 2sg of √कृ ('you will do/perform'); the object इमम् धर्म्यम् सङ्ग्रामम् (द्वितीया एकवचन, all agreeing). The apodosis: अवाप्स्यसि (अव-√आप्, लृट् 2sg, 'you will incur') — पापम् (sin) as the object.

2.33.title: हित्वा — absolutive of doubling losses
2.33.body: हित्वा is absolutive of √हा ('having abandoned'). It governs two द्वितीया एकवचन objects joined by च: स्वधर्मम् and कीर्तिम् (feminine -इ stem). The grammar bundles the two losses into one act of abandonment — to skip the battle is to drop both duty and reputation in one move.

2.34.title: अव्ययाम् — feminine accusative of -अव्यय
2.34.body: अव्ययाम् is स्त्रीलिंग द्वितीया एकवचन (the -आ feminine of अव्यय 'imperishable'), agreeing with अकीर्तिम् (द्वितीया एकवचन of अकीर्ति, feminine -ई/-इ stem). कथयिष्यन्ति (कथय् causative of √कथ् in लृट् 3pl — 'they will narrate') governs both objects: तव (षष्ठी) and अव्ययाम् अकीर्तिम्.

2.34.title: अतिरिच्यते — passive of comparison
2.34.body: अतिरिच्यते (अति-√रिच्, करणि लट् 3sg, 'is exceeded/surpasses') governs मरणात् (पञ्चमी एकवचन — 'than death'). The subject is अकीर्तिः (प्रथमा एकवचन, feminine). सम्भावितस्य is षष्ठी एकवचन of past passive participle (सम्-√भू णिच् — 'of one who has been honoured'). The grammar quantifies dishonour: for the honoured, it exceeds death.

2.35.title: मंस्यन्ते — future middle of √मन्
2.35.body: मंस्यन्ते is लृट् आत्मनेपद 3pl of √मन् ('they will think') — note the -स्य- future infix and the आत्मनेपद ending -न्ते. भयात् रणात् उपरतम् (तव) — पञ्चमी एकवचन ablatives + past passive participle (उप-√रम् 'withdrawn'). The construction: 'they will think you withdrawn from battle out of fear'.

2.35.title: Future यास्यसि — going-to-lightness
2.35.body: यास्यसि is लृट् 2sg of √या ('you will go'); the object लाघवम् (द्वितीया एकवचन of अकर्मधारय abstract noun — 'lightness, contempt'). भूत्वा is absolutive of √भू ('having been'). बहुमतः (प्रथमा एकवचन — 'highly esteemed', past participle of बहु-√मन्) is the predicate of भूत्वा. The grammar tracks reputation's full collapse: esteemed → light.

2.36.title: अवाच्यवादान् — compound of forbidden speech
2.36.body: अवाच्यवादान् is द्वितीया बहुवचन — कर्मधारय of अवाच्य (कृत्य-कृदन्त of √वच् with नञ्, 'unspeakable') and वाद ('speech'). वदिष्यन्ति is लृट् 3pl of √वद् ('they will speak'). तव अहिताः ('your enemies', प्रथमा बहुवचन) — अहित = नञ् + हित, where हित is past passive participle of √धा ('placed, beneficial'); negated, 'unfriendly / enemy'.

2.36.title: Rhetorical दुःखतरम् with interrogative
2.36.body: निन्दन्तः is शतृ-कृदन्त (present active participle of √निन्द्, प्रथमा बहुवचन — 'reviling'). The interrogative ततः दुःखतरम् नु किम् — 'than that, what is more painful?' — uses ततः (ablative-adverb), दुःखतरम् (नपुंसकलिङ्ग प्रथमा एकवचन, comparative), and nu-kim as the rhetorical question particles. Grammar mirrors content: even the morphology screams worse-than-this.

2.37.title: Two past participles as conditions
2.37.body: हतः (past passive participle of √हन्, प्रथमा एकवचन — 'killed') and the absolutive जित्वा (√जि — 'having conquered') are the two alternatives. प्राप्स्यसि (प्र-√आप्, लृट् 2sg) and भोक्ष्यसे (√भुज्, लृट् आत्मनेपद 2sg — note middle voice for self-benefit) are the futures. The grammar mirrors win/loss in active/middle voice.

2.37.title: कृतनिश्चयः — past participle as resolution
2.37.body: कृतनिश्चयः is बहुव्रीहि ('whose resolution is made'), प्रथमा एकवचन agreeing with implied त्वम् of उत्तिष्ठ (ut-√स्था लोट् 2sg). युद्धाय is चतुर्थी एकवचन — dative of purpose ('for battle'). The closing reuses the 2.3 imperative उत्तिष्ठ but with a different motivation: not psychology but pragmatics.

2.38.title: Three द्वन्द्व pairs reduced to equality
2.38.body: सुखदुःखे (नपुंसक प्रथमा द्विवचन dvandva), लाभालाभौ (पुल्लिङ्ग प्रथमा द्विवचन dvandva), जयाजयौ (पुल्लिङ्ग प्रथमा द्विवचन dvandva) — three duals collapse the six oppositions. कृत्वा (absolutive of √कृ) governs them as predicates with समे/समान् ('equal' agreeing in द्वितीया द्विवचन).

2.38.title: Closing युज्यस्व + future result
2.38.body: युज्यस्व is लोट् आत्मनेपद 2sg of √युज् — 'yoke yourself' (to battle, युद्धाय चतुर्थी). The verb forecasts the Yoga half of the chapter: literally, this is the first imperative of yoga in the Gītā. एवम् पापम् न अवाप्स्यसि (लृट् 2sg) — 'thus you will not incur sin' — the future negation closes the kṣatriya argument.

2.39.title: Saṅkhya / Yoga distinction made grammatical
2.39.body: एषा (स्त्रीलिंग प्रथमा एकवचन of एतद्) refers to बुद्धिः (feminine). अभिहिता (past passive participle of अभि-√धा, feminine नी-आ) — 'has been declared'. साङ्ख्ये (सप्तमी एकवचन — 'in Sāṅkhya') is the locative of domain; योगे तु (locative, contrastive) names the second domain. इमाम् (द्वितीया एकवचन feminine) — the new one to come.

2.39.title: Future प्रहास्यसि — yoga's specific outcome
2.39.body: प्रहास्यसि is लृट् 2sg of प्र-√हा ('you will give up'). The object is कर्मबन्धम् (तत्पुरुष 'karma-bondage', द्वितीया एकवचन). The yoga-buddhi is yoked: युक्तः (past passive participle of √युज्, प्रथमा एकवचन) agreeing with implied त्वम्. यया (तृतीया एकवचन feminine of यद्) — instrumental of the बुद्धि by which one acts.

2.40.title: Two negated abstracts in pāda 1
2.40.body: अभिक्रमनाशः ('the destruction of an undertaking', तत्पुरुष) and प्रत्यवायः ('adverse-effect', उपसर्ग-based) — both प्रथमा एकवचन subjects negated by न. अस्ति and विद्यते are both present-tense existentials (लट् 3sg). इह ('here, in this [yoga]') locates the assurance.

2.40.title: स्वल्पम् अपि...त्रायते — minimal-action benefit
2.40.body: स्वल्पम् अपि ('even a little', नपुंसक प्रथमा एकवचन) is the subject of त्रायते (√त्रै, करणि-शुद्ध्या लट् आत्मनेपद 3sg — 'protects, saves'). अस्य धर्मस्य (षष्ठी एकवचन — 'of this dharma'). महतः भयात् (पञ्चमी एकवचन of comparative महत् — 'from a great fear') is the ablative-of-removal. The grammar makes it a guarantee, not encouragement.

2.41.title: Singular vs. plural बुद्धि as structural opposition
2.41.body: व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिः एका (प्रथमा एकवचन feminine) — 'the resolute intelligence is one'. व्यवसायात्मिका is बहुव्रीहि ('whose essence is determination'); -आत्मिका is the feminine of -आत्मक. Against this: बुद्धयः (प्रथमा बहुवचन — 'intelligences', plural) of अव्यवसायिनाम् (षष्ठी बहुवचन of -इन् compound 'of the indecisive').

2.41.title: बहुशाखाः अनन्ताः — two feminine adjectives
2.41.body: बहुशाखाः (बहुव्रीहि 'many-branched', feminine प्रथमा बहुवचन) and अनन्ताः ('endless') agree with बुद्धयः. हि ('for, indeed') is causal. The botanical metaphor — one trunk vs. many branches — sits in adjective morphology. The vocative कुरुनन्दन ('joy of the Kurus', तत्पुरुष) personalizes the address.

2.42.title: पुष्पिताम् वाचम् — feminine accusative with metaphor
2.42.body: इमाम् पुष्पिताम् वाचम् (all स्त्रीलिंग द्वितीया एकवचन) — 'this flowery speech'. पुष्पिताम् is past participle of denominative पुष्पय् ('flowered'). प्रवदन्ति is लट् 3pl of प्र-√वद् ('they utter'). अविपश्चितः ('the un-discerning', प्रथमा बहुवचन of विपश्चित् with नञ्) is the subject.

2.42.title: वेदवादरताः — bahuvrīhi labelling the literalists
2.42.body: वेदवादरताः ('attached to Veda-recitations', बहुव्रीहि — वेद-वाद-रत — 'whose delight is in Veda-words'), प्रथमा बहुवचन, in apposition to अविपश्चितः. नान्यद् अस्ति इति वादिनः — 'saying that there is nothing else'; वादिनः is प्रथमा बहुवचन of वादिन् (-इन् compound, 'speakers'); इति is the quote-marker.

2.43.title: Five accusative-feminine adjectives in series
2.43.body: कामात्मानः, स्वर्गपराः — प्रथमा बहुवचन subjects (बहुव्रीहि compounds). Then accusative feminine objects modifying the implied वाचम्/बुद्धिम् from 2.42: जन्मकर्मफलप्रदाम् (तत्पुरुष: 'giving birth-as-action-result'), क्रियाविशेषबहुलाम् ('rich in particulars of action'). All स्त्रीलिंग द्वितीया एकवचन agreeing with the implied feminine.

2.43.title: प्रति — postposition governing accusative
2.43.body: भोगैश्वर्यगतिम् प्रति — 'aimed at (the goal of) enjoyments-and-lordships'. प्रति postposes the द्वितीया एकवचन feminine. The verse is grammatically incomplete — it dangles from 2.42 (वाचम्). Sanskrit's compound-stacking allows philosophical critique to be built morphologically: each compound is a diagnosis.

2.44.title: Two षष्ठी बहुवचन participles describing the diverted
2.44.body: भोगैश्वर्यप्रसक्तानाम् ('of those attached to enjoyment-and-lordship', past participle of प्र-√सञ्ज् in षष्ठी बहुवचन) and तया अपहृतचेतसाम् (बहुव्रीहि 'whose minds are stolen by it', षष्ठी बहुवचन, with तया = तृतीया एकवचन feminine referring back to the speech of 2.42).

2.44.title: विधीयते — passive of placement
2.44.body: व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिः (subject, प्रथमा एकवचन feminine) न विधीयते (वि-√धा, करणि लट् 3sg — 'is not placed'). समाधौ is सप्तमी एकवचन of समाधि (locative — 'in samādhi'). Grammar: the determinate intellect is not located in samādhi for such people. The passive distances agency — it isn't refused, it doesn't form.

2.45.title: Five predicate adjectives governed by भव
2.45.body: Pāda 2: निस्त्रैगुण्यः, निर्द्वन्द्वः, नित्यसत्त्वस्थः, निर्योगक्षेमः, आत्मवान् — all पुल्लिङ्ग प्रथमा एकवचन predicate adjectives governed by भव (लोट् 2sg of √भू, 'become!'). The chain of न-prefixes (निस्/निर्) is grammatical scaffolding — 'be beyond-the-three-guṇas, beyond-pairs-of-opposites'. The vocative is अर्जुन alone; the five -निः/-वान् forms are predicates of the imperative.

2.45.title: त्रैगुण्यविषयाः वेदाः — subject and predicate
2.45.body: वेदाः (प्रथमा बहुवचन) is subject; त्रैगुण्यविषयाः ('whose object is the three-guṇa-domain', बहुव्रीहि) is predicate. The diagnosis: the Vedas (the ritual portion implied) operate within the three-guṇa-domain. The verse pivots from describing Veda to addressing Arjuna with five negations — descriptive → imperative.

2.46.title: यावत्/तावत् correlative of proportion
2.46.body: यावान् (प्रथमा एकवचन of यावत् — adjectival relative) and तावान् (correlative) — agreeing with अर्थः ('purpose, use'). उदपाने is सप्तमी एकवचन ('in a well'); सम्प्लुतोदके is सप्तमी एकवचन ('in flooded waters') agreeing with implied स्थाने. The locative absolute structure: 'as much use in a well, when there is flooding-of-water everywhere'.

2.46.title: ब्राह्मणस्य विजानतः — षष्ठी एकवचन with present participle
2.46.body: ब्राह्मणस्य is षष्ठी एकवचन; विजानतः is the शतृ-कृदन्त (present active participle) of वि-√ज्ञा in षष्ठी एकवचन ('of one who fully knows'). The construction: 'for the knowing Brahmin, that much use is in all the Vedas'. The morphology makes 'knower' a participial qualifier of the genitive.

2.47.title: Three मा-prohibitives covering three wrong stances
2.47.body: Pāda 1: कर्मणि एव अधिकारः ते — सप्तमी एकवचन of कर्मन् + nominative + ते (षष्ठी एकवचन enclitic of युष्मद्, 'your right'), with implicit अस्ति. Then three मा-prohibitives: मा फलेषु (locative — 'never in fruits', implicitly with अधिकारः); मा कर्मफलहेतुः भूः (मा + √भू लुङ् 2sg — augmentless aorist with मा); मा सङ्गः अस्तु अकर्मणि (मा + लोट् 3sg of √अस् + सप्तमी एकवचन).

2.47.title: Three locatives of false attachment
2.47.body: कर्मणि (सप्तमी एकवचन of कर्मन् — 'in action'), फलेषु (सप्तमी बहुवचन of फल — 'in fruits'), अकर्मणि (सप्तमी एकवचन — 'in inaction'). Sanskrit's locative-of-domain pinpoints each wrong attachment-zone. The grammar's geometry: right→fruits→inaction. कर्मफलहेतुः is बहुव्रीहि ('whose motive is karma's fruit').

2.48.title: योगस्थः कुरु — imperative anchored by adjective
2.48.body: योगस्थः (तत्पुरुष: 'standing in yoga', प्रथमा एकवचन) is the predicate adjective with implied भव. कुरु (लोट् 2sg of √कृ) governs कर्माणि (नपुंसक द्वितीया बहुवचन — 'actions'). त्यक्त्वा is absolutive ('having abandoned') with object सङ्गम् (द्वितीया एकवचन — 'attachment'). The grammar layers: in-yoga (state), having-abandoned (sequence), do (command).

2.48.title: समत्वम् योगः उच्यते — yoga's definition
2.48.body: Closing equation: समत्वम् ('equanimity', नपुंसक प्रथमा एकवचन abstract noun) योगः (प्रथमा एकवचन masculine) उच्यते (passive of √वच्, करणि लट् 3sg — 'is called'). सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योः is षष्ठी द्विवचन (dual genitive of dvandva — 'of success-and-failure'). भूत्वा absolutive of √भू. Three verb-forms, one definitional copula.

2.49.title: दूरेण ह्यवरम् — instrumental of separation
2.49.body: दूरेण is तृतीया एकवचन ('by a distance, by far') — instrumental of degree. अवरम् कर्म ('inferior action', नपुंसक प्रथमा एकवचन) is the subject; बुद्धियोगात् (पञ्चमी एकवचन — 'than buddhi-yoga') is ablative of comparison. The grammar quantifies inferiority instrumentally and ablatively in one breath.

2.49.title: Imperative अन्विच्छ + locative बुद्धौ
2.49.body: अन्विच्छ (अनु-√इष्, लोट् 2sg — 'seek') governs शरणम् (द्वितीया एकवचन — 'refuge'). बुद्धौ is सप्तमी एकवचन — 'in intelligence'. कृपणाः फलहेतवः — predicate phrase: 'wretched are the fruit-motivated ones'; फलहेतवः is बहुव्रीहि (प्रथमा बहुवचन — '-हेतु' stems).

2.50.title: जहाति — present 3sg of √हा
2.50.body: जहाति is लट् 3sg of √हा ('he abandons'), formed from the जुहोत्यादि (3rd class, athematic) reduplicative stem ज-हा- + ending -ति. The object उभे सुकृतदुष्कृते (नपुंसक प्रथमा द्विवचन — 'both, the good-done and bad-done') is a द्वन्द्व-dual. बुद्धियुक्तः is तृतीया-तत्पुरुष with past participle head (प्रथमा एकवचन — 'one yoked with buddhi').

2.50.title: तस्मात् + युज्यस्व + definition
2.50.body: तस्मात् (ablative-adverb 'therefore') with लोट् युज्यस्व (√युज् आत्मनेपद 2sg — 'yoke yourself'). योगाय is चतुर्थी एकवचन (dative of purpose — 'for yoga'). Closing aphorism: योगः कर्मसु कौशलम् — 'yoga is skill in actions'; कर्मसु is सप्तमी बहुवचन (locative of domain), कौशलम् is नपुंसकलिङ्ग abstract noun.

2.51.title: Two participles describing the buddhi-yoked
2.51.body: बुद्धियुक्ताः (past participle compound, प्रथमा बहुवचन) and मनीषिणः ('wise ones', प्रथमा बहुवचन of मनीषिन्). त्यक्त्वा is absolutive of √त्यज् with object फलम् (कर्मजम् — 'born of action', कर्मधारय). The grammar mirrors 2.50 in plural: same operation done by many minds.

2.51.title: Compound जन्मबन्धविनिर्मुक्ताः
2.51.body: जन्मबन्धविनिर्मुक्ताः is a long तत्पुरुष + past participle: 'released from the bondage of birth' (वि-निर्-√मुच्), प्रथमा बहुवचन agreeing with मनीषिणः. पदम् अनामयम् ('the disease-free state', कर्मधारय — अनामय = नञ् + आमय 'disease') is द्वितीया एकवचन, object of गच्छन्ति (√गम्, लट् 3pl).

2.52.title: मोहकलिलम् — the dense compound noun
2.52.body: मोहकलिलम् ('the thicket of delusion', तत्पुरुष, नपुंसक एकवचन) is the object of व्यतितरिष्यति (वि-अति-√तृ, लृट् 3sg — 'will cross over'). The subject is ते बुद्धिः (षष्ठी एकवचन + प्रथमा). The यदा-clause sets the temporal threshold.

2.52.title: गन्तासि — periphrastic future of √गम्
2.52.body: गन्तासि is the लुट् (periphrastic future) 2sg of √गम् — 'you will go' (the rare second-person form: agent-noun गन्ता + असि). The object निर्वेदम् (द्वितीया एकवचन — 'disgust, dispassion'); the qualifiers श्रोतव्यस्य and श्रुतस्य are षष्ठी एकवचन (कृत्य and past participle of √श्रु — 'of what is to-be-heard and what has-been-heard').

2.53.title: श्रुतिविप्रतिपन्ना — feminine past participle compound
2.53.body: श्रुतिविप्रतिपन्ना (तत्पुरुष + वि-प्रति-√पद् past participle, स्त्रीलिंग प्रथमा एकवचन — 'shaken/diverted by śruti') agrees with बुद्धिः (feminine). The clause's verb: स्थास्यति निश्चला (लृट् 3sg of √स्था + adjective in feminine — 'will stand unmoving'). समाधौ is सप्तमी एकवचन (locative — 'in samādhi').

2.53.title: अवाप्स्यसि — future of accomplishment
2.53.body: अवाप्स्यसि (अव-√आप्, लृट् 2sg — 'you will obtain') governs योगम् (द्वितीया एकवचन). The यदा...तदा correlative locks the temporal sequence: when buddhi-stands-still in samādhi, then yoga is obtained. The verb is the same अवाप् that appeared in 2.33 (incurring sin) — here re-purposed for the positive obtaining.

2.54.title: Arjuna's four interrogatives, three of them optative
2.54.body: Pāda 2 onwards: का भाषा (interrogative feminine + feminine noun — 'what speech?', with implicit अस्ति); किम् प्रभाषेत (विधिलिङ् 3sg of प्र-√भाष् — 'what would he speak?'); किम् आसीत (विधिलिङ् 3sg of √आस् — 'how would he sit?'); व्रजेत किम् (विधिलिङ् 3sg of √व्रज् — 'how would he walk?'). Four interrogatives, three of them विधिलिङ् — all asking how the स्थितप्रज्ञ outwardly behaves.

2.54.title: Two षष्ठी एकवचन compound titles
2.54.body: स्थितप्रज्ञस्य and समाधिस्थस्य — both षष्ठी एकवचन of long compounds. स्थितप्रज्ञ is बहुव्रीहि ('whose wisdom is established'); समाधिस्थ is तत्पुरुष ('standing in samādhi'). Arjuna asks for the lakṣaṇa (mark) of this person — Kṛṣṇa's answer fills 2.55 to 2.72.

2.55.title: प्रजहाति + द्वितीया बहुवचन of cravings
2.55.body: प्रजहाति (प्र-√हा, लट् 3sg — 'gives up') governs कामान् सर्वान् मनोगतान् (all पुल्लिङ्ग द्वितीया बहुवचन agreeing — 'all the mind-gone desires'). मनोगतान् is past participle compound (तत्पुरुष: 'gone to the mind'). The यदा-clause supplies the trigger.

2.55.title: आत्मनि एव आत्मना तुष्टः
2.55.body: आत्मनि is सप्तमी एकवचन ('in the self'); आत्मना is तृतीया एकवचन ('by/with the self'); तुष्टः is past passive participle of √तुष् (प्रथमा एकवचन). The triple-case आत्मन् is a deliberate locative-instrumental-nominative loop. उच्यते (passive of √वच्, लट् 3sg) closes — 'he is called' (स्थितप्रज्ञ).

2.56.title: Three bahuvrīhi compounds for one definition
2.56.body: दुःखेषु अनुद्विग्नमनाः ('whose mind is unagitated in sorrows', सप्तमी + बहुव्रीहि), सुखेषु विगतस्पृहः ('whose desire has departed in pleasures'), वीतरागभयक्रोधः ('whose passion-fear-anger have gone' — a triple-dvandva inside the bahuvrīhi). All three प्रथमा एकवचन, predicates of स्थितधीः मुनिः.

2.56.title: स्थितधीः vs. स्थितप्रज्ञः — synonym shift
2.56.body: स्थितधीः (बहुव्रीहि 'whose intellect is established') replaces 2.55's स्थितप्रज्ञः. The two -धी and -प्रज्ञा morphological choices are nearly identical — Sanskrit's preference for varied compounds (वैचित्र्य) rather than repetition. उच्यते (passive present 3sg) is the recurring verb of naming.

2.57.title: अनभिस्नेहः — the negated emotion-compound
2.57.body: अनभिस्नेहः is नञ्-तत्पुरुष ('without affection-towards') in प्रथमा एकवचन. सर्वत्र is indeclinable locative-adverb ('everywhere'). तत्तत् is duplicated demonstrative — 'this and that' (whatever comes). प्राप्य is the absolutive of प्र-√आप् ('having obtained'). Two finite verbs: नाभिनन्दति (न + अभि-√नन्द्, लट् 3sg — 'does not rejoice') and न द्वेष्टि (√द्विष्, लट् 3sg — 'does not hate').

2.57.title: प्रतिष्ठिता — feminine perfect-passive participle
2.57.body: तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता — (तस्य षष्ठी + प्रज्ञा feminine subject + प्रतिष्ठिता past passive participle of प्रति-√स्था in feminine प्रथमा एकवचन — 'established'). This formula 'tasya prajñā pratiṣṭhitā' becomes the chapter's refrain (recurs 2.58, 2.61, 2.68). Grammatically, the participle stands in for the copula — 'his wisdom [is] established'.

2.58.title: कूर्मः इव — comparative simile via इव
2.58.body: कूर्मः (प्रथमा एकवचन — 'tortoise') with इव ('like'). अङ्गानि (नपुंसक द्वितीया बहुवचन — 'limbs') is the object of an implied सम्हरते applied to the tortoise. The actual subject: अयम् (the wise one) with संहरते (सम्-√हृ, लट् आत्मनेपद 3sg — 'withdraws'). सर्वशः is indeclinable adverb ('on all sides').

2.58.title: इन्द्रियाणि इन्द्रियार्थेभ्यः — paired cases
2.58.body: इन्द्रियाणि is नपुंसक द्वितीया बहुवचन (object — 'the senses'); इन्द्रियार्थेभ्यः is पञ्चमी बहुवचन (ablative — 'from the sense-objects'). The case-pairing names the act: withdraw senses *from* their objects. The refrain तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता closes.

2.59.title: विषयाः विनिवर्तन्ते — middle voice of receding
2.59.body: विषयाः (प्रथमा बहुवचन — 'objects') subjects of विनिवर्तन्ते (वि-नि-√वृत्, लट् आत्मनेपद 3pl — 'turn back/withdraw'). निराहारस्य देहिनः is षष्ठी एकवचन ('of the abstaining embodied one' — निराहार is बहुव्रीहि 'without food'). रसवर्जम् is adverbial accusative ('with the exception of taste').

2.59.title: परम् दृष्ट्वा — the absolutive of vision
2.59.body: रसः अपि अस्य ('but the taste, of him') — परम् दृष्ट्वा (absolutive of √दृश् — 'having seen the Supreme') — निवर्तते (नि-√वृत्, लट् आत्मनेपद 3sg — 'turns back'). The grammar makes the exception precise: even rasa goes, but only on perceiving परम्. The absolutive sequences the cognition before the result.

2.60.title: Two षष्ठी एकवचन agreeing genitives
2.60.body: यततः and विपश्चितः are both षष्ठी एकवचन — agent genitive with possessive participle force ('of the striving, of the discerning'). पुरुषस्य fits between them. यततः is the शानच्/शतृ-कृदन्त of यत् (active participle in षष्ठी). The construction: 'of the striving discerning man, even him...'.

2.60.title: प्रमाथीनि — neuter plural of an -इन् adjective
2.60.body: प्रमाथीनि is नपुंसक प्रथमा बहुवचन of प्रमाथिन् ('agitating', from प्र-√मथ्), agreeing with इन्द्रियाणि. हरन्ति (√हृ, लट् 3pl — 'they carry off') governs मनः (द्वितीया एकवचन of मनस्). प्रसभम् is adverbial ('forcibly'). Grammar: forcibly-carry-off-mind. The senses do this to even the wise.

2.61.title: Three layers of object-handling in one verse
2.61.body: तानि सर्वाणि (नपुंसक द्वितीया बहुवचन — 'all those', referring to इन्द्रियाणि) is the object of संयम्य (absolutive of सम्-√यम् — 'having restrained'). युक्तः (past participle, प्रथमा एकवचन) is predicate adjective. आसीत is विधिलिङ् 3sg of √आस् ('let him sit'). मत्परः is बहुव्रीहि ('whose para is me') — predicate.

2.61.title: वशे...यस्य इन्द्रियाणि — locative of control
2.61.body: वशे is सप्तमी एकवचन ('in control'); यस्य is षष्ठी एकवचन of यद्. The construction यस्य इन्द्रियाणि वशे (हि) — 'whose senses are in control'. The chapter-refrain closes: तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता. The grammar makes mastery a locative state, not an action.

2.62.title: षष्ठी एकवचन + locative participle ध्यायतः
2.62.body: ध्यायतः is the शतृ-कृदन्त of √ध्यै in षष्ठी एकवचन ('of one meditating'). पुंसः is also षष्ठी एकवचन ('of a man'). विषयान् is द्वितीया बहुवचन — object of the participle. सङ्गः ('attachment', प्रथमा एकवचन) is the subject of उपजायते (उप-√जन्, लट् आत्मनेपद 3sg — 'arises').

2.62.title: The famous chain of ablatives
2.62.body: सङ्गात् (पञ्चमी एकवचन — 'from attachment') → कामः (lat. of सञ्जायते — सम्-√जन् लट् आत्मनेपद 3sg — 'is born'). कामात् → क्रोधः अभिजायते (अभि-√जन् लट् 3sg). The ablative-chain syntactically derives one emotion from the previous — Sanskrit's case-system makes causality grammatical.

2.63.title: Five-step decline encoded in five ablatives
2.63.body: क्रोधात् → सम्मोहः; सम्मोहात् → स्मृतिविभ्रमः; स्मृतिभ्रंशात् → बुद्धिनाशः; बुद्धिनाशात् → प्रणश्यति. Each ablative (पञ्चमी एकवचन) is the source; each nominative (प्रथमा एकवचन) the result. भवति, स्मृतिविभ्रमः (implicit), and the final verb प्रणश्यति (प्र-√नश्, लट् 3sg — 'he perishes').

2.63.title: Three तत्पुरुष compounds inside the chain
2.63.body: स्मृतिविभ्रमः (तत्पुरुष — 'confusion of memory'), स्मृतिभ्रंशः (तत्पुरुष — 'loss of memory'), बुद्धिनाशः (तत्पुरुष — 'destruction of intellect') — three compounds at the chain's center. The morphology compresses each step into a single noun. The final प्रणश्यति lacks an explicit subject — the whole person is implied: he himself perishes.

2.64.title: रागद्वेषवियुक्तैः — instrumental plural participle
2.64.body: रागद्वेषवियुक्तैः is तृतीया बहुवचन (past participle of वि-√युज् with द्वन्द्व prefix — 'separated from passion and aversion'). It agrees with इन्द्रियैः (तृतीया बहुवचन of इन्द्रिय — 'with the senses'). आत्मवश्यैः similarly तृतीया बहुवचन ('with self-controlled [senses]'). चरन् is शतृ-कृदन्त (active participle, प्रथमा एकवचन — 'moving among').

2.64.title: विधेयात्मा — the disciplined-soul compound
2.64.body: विधेयात्मा is बहुव्रीहि ('whose self is to-be-directed', कृत्य of वि-√धा + आत्मन्), प्रथमा एकवचन — predicate subject. प्रसादम् (द्वितीया एकवचन — 'serenity') is the object of अधिगच्छति (अधि-√गम्, लट् 3sg — 'attains'). The grammar pairs disciplined-self with attained-serenity.

2.65.title: Locative प्रसादे starting the verse
2.65.body: प्रसादे is सप्तमी एकवचन — 'in [the state of] serenity'. हानिः (प्रथमा एकवचन feminine — 'cessation, loss') is the subject of उपजायते (उप-√जन्, लट् आत्मनेपद 3sg). सर्वदुःखानाम् is षष्ठी बहुवचन ('of all sorrows'). अस्य is षष्ठी एकवचन referring to विधेयात्मा.

2.65.title: प्रसन्नचेतसः + पर्यवतिष्ठते
2.65.body: प्रसन्नचेतसः is षष्ठी एकवचन of बहुव्रीहि ('of one whose mind is serene'). आशु is indeclinable adverb ('quickly'). बुद्धिः (subject) पर्यवतिष्ठते (परि-अव-√स्था, लट् आत्मनेपद 3sg — 'firmly establishes itself'). The middle voice signals self-action — the intellect settles on its own once the conditions are met.

2.66.title: Four-stage negation of the un-yoked
2.66.body: Four sequential negations: नास्ति बुद्धिः अयुक्तस्य (न-अस्ति + षष्ठी एकवचन of अयुक्त — 'no intellect for the unyoked'); न चायुक्तस्य भावना ('no contemplation'); न च अभावयतः शान्तिः (षष्ठी एकवचन of शतृ-कृदन्त of णिजन्त भावय् — 'of the non-contemplating'); अशान्तस्य कुतः सुखम्? (rhetorical, ablative-interrogative).

2.66.title: Genitive cascade with implied अस्ति
2.66.body: Each clause repeats the pattern: षष्ठी एकवचन (of the failed agent) + प्रथमा एकवचन (the absent quality). The implied verb अस्ति is dropped each time — Sanskrit's stylistic preference for ellipsis in chains. The closing कुतः सुखम् is interrogative-ablative: 'whence pleasure?' — the answer is morphologically absent because the thing is.

2.67.title: Genitive-of-roaming + relative-correlative
2.67.body: इन्द्रियाणाम् चरताम् (षष्ठी बहुवचन of शतृ-कृदन्त of √चर् — 'of the roaming senses') is the head genitive. यन्मनः अनुविधीयते — यद् + मनः + अनु-वि-√धा (passive लट् 3sg — 'whichever [sense] the mind follows after'). The yad-tad correlative is held inside a participle-construction.

2.67.title: वायुः नावम् इव — boat-and-wind simile
2.67.body: तत् हरति प्रज्ञाम् (तत् refers back; हरति is लट् 3sg of √हृ — 'carries off intelligence'). वायुः नावम् इव अम्भसि — 'as wind [carries] a boat on the waters'; नावम् is द्वितीया एकवचन feminine (irregular नौ stem); अम्भसि is सप्तमी एकवचन of अम्भस्. The simile is parallel-case-marked: object-on-locative.

2.68.title: निगृहीतानि — past passive participle in neuter plural
2.68.body: निगृहीतानि (नि-√ग्रह् past participle, नपुंसक प्रथमा बहुवचन — 'restrained') agrees with इन्द्रियाणि. यस्य ('whose', षष्ठी एकवचन) is the relative; तस्य (the correlative) introduces the refrain. सर्वशः indeclinable ('on all sides').

2.68.title: Chapter-refrain's fourth iteration
2.68.body: तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता — the refrain (also at 2.57, 2.58, 2.61) closes the section on senses. The repetition is structural: Kṛṣṇa demarcates one criterion per verse, ends each with the same morphological seal. इन्द्रियार्थेभ्यः is पञ्चमी बहुवचन — paired with द्वितीया इन्द्रियाणि in case-symmetry with 2.58.

2.69.title: Two relative pronouns of inverted vision
2.69.body: या निशा (feminine रेलाटिवे — 'what is night') and यस्याम् (locative feminine — 'in which') anchor pādas 1 and 3. The सर्वभूतानाम् (षष्ठी बहुवचन — 'of all beings') is the genitive. जागर्ति is लट् 3sg of √जागृ ('he wakes'); संयमी is the subject (-इन् compound, प्रथमा एकवचन).

2.69.title: पश्यतः मुनेः — षष्ठी एकवचन of seer
2.69.body: पश्यतः is षष्ठी एकवचन of शतृ-कृदन्त of √दृश् ('of the seeing'); मुनेः is also षष्ठी एकवचन. The construction: 'that [what is day for them] is night for the seeing sage'. The Sanskrit chiasmus is exact: यस्याम् जाग्रति भूतानि (locative-plural participle) :: सा निशा पश्यतः मुनेः.

2.70.title: Long त्रिष्टुभ् simile via आपूर्यमाणम्
2.70.body: आपूर्यमाणम् is present passive participle of आ-√पॄ (नपुंसक द्वितीया एकवचन — 'being filled') agreeing with समुद्रम् ('ocean'). अचलप्रतिष्ठम् is बहुव्रीहि ('whose foundation is unmoving'). आपः (only-plural feminine) is the subject of प्रविशन्ति (प्र-√विश्, लट् 3pl — 'enter'). Two relative-correlative connectors: यद्वत्...तद्वत्.

2.70.title: Closing contrast न कामकामी
2.70.body: तत्-वत् कामाः (नपुंसक प्रथमा बहुवचन of काम) यम् प्रविशन्ति सर्वे — relative-clause + object. सः शान्तिम् आप्नोति (√आप् लट् 3sg) — 'he obtains peace'. The final न कामकामी ('not the desire-desirer', बहुव्रीहि agent-noun of कामय्) is the explicit contrast — the receptacle metaphor only fits the renouncer, not the seeker.

2.71.title: Three bahuvrīhi negations in a row
2.71.body: निःस्पृहः (नञ्-तत्पुरुष or बहुव्रीहि — 'without longing'), निर्ममः ('without mine-ness'), निरहङ्कारः ('without I-ness') — all प्रथमा एकवचन agreeing with the subject पुमान् (irregular: nom. sg. of पुम्स् 'man'). The -निः prefix is the morphological signature of renunciation.

2.71.title: विहाय...चरति — absolutive + finite
2.71.body: विहाय is the absolutive of वि-√हा ('having abandoned'); object कामान् सर्वान् (द्वितीया बहुवचन). चरति is लट् 3sg of √चर् ('he moves'). शान्तिम् अधिगच्छति (अधि-√गम्, लट् 3sg — 'attains peace'). The grammar sequences: having-abandoned → moves-about → attains. Yoga is a verb in present tense, not an aspiration.

2.72.title: ब्राह्मी स्थितिः — feminine demonstrative
2.72.body: एषा ब्राह्मी स्थितिः — all स्त्रीलिंग प्रथमा एकवचन ('this is the Brahmic state'). ब्राह्मी is the feminine derivative of ब्रह्मन् with -अण/-ई. एनाम् (द्वितीया एकवचन feminine of एतद्) is the object of प्राप्य (absolutive of प्र-√आप्). विमुह्यति (वि-√मुह्, लट् 3sg — 'is deluded') is the matrix verb, negated.

2.72.title: स्थित्वा + अन्तकाले + ऋच्छति
2.72.body: स्थित्वा is absolutive of √स्था ('having stood'); अस्याम् is सप्तमी एकवचन feminine — 'in this [state]'. अन्तकाले is सप्तमी एकवचन ('at the end-time'); अपि concessive. ऋच्छति is लट् 3sg of √ऋ ('reaches'); ब्रह्मनिर्वाणम् is object (द्वितीया एकवचन of तत्पुरुष — 'Brahma-extinction'). The chapter's last verb is attainment; the chapter's last word is निर्वाणम्.
